title: Chaos to Synchronization and Dissipative Quantum Scarring in Open Coupled top-Dicke model in a Lossy Cavity
authors: Debabrata Mondal, Sohan Pati, S. Sinha
abstract: We present a variant of the Dicke model, termed as the open coupled-top Dicke model, which enables the exploration of rich non-equilibrium phenomena, particularly the fate of quantum scars in an open environment. This model can effectively be realized by coupling a two-species Bose-Josephson junction to a lossy cavity. Photon loss induces spontaneous synchronization via projection onto a dissipation-free subspace, along with transient chaos followed by restoration of synchronization and coherence. We identify two distinct scarring phenomena in the presence of dissipation. One remains protected, exhibiting persistent revivals, while the scar associated with the superradiant phase displays a dissipation-induced slow decay of the survival probability. Remarkably, for sufficiently small spin magnitude, the chaos-assisted macroscopic quantum tunneling is linked to the latter type of scarring. The results can be readily tested in ongoing cavity QED experiments and have broader applicability in other platforms.
summary: This paper explores the dynamics of an open Dicke model where photon loss drives the system toward synchronization and regulates transient chaos. It identifies two distinct classes of dissipative quantum scars, one of which is protected from decay. The findings are relevant for controlling many-body states in cavity QED and Bose-Josephson junction platforms.
main_problem: Investigating how dissipation and photon loss influence non-equilibrium phenomena, specifically the stability of many-body quantum scars and the transition from chaos to synchronization.
main_result: The study identifies two types of dissipative quantum scarring: a protected type with persistent oscillations and a dissipative type with slow decay, while showing that photon loss induces spontaneous synchronization and suppresses transient chaos.
method: The authors use a combination of classical stability analysis of equations of motion and quantum simulations via the stochastic wave-function (quantum trajectories) method within a Lindblad master equation framework.
model_or_system: An open coupled-top Dicke model (OCTD) consisting of two interacting large spins coupled to a lossy cavity mode, which can be physically realized by a two-species Bose-Josephson junction in a cavity.
key_observables: Photon number, spin components, population imbalance, relative phase, survival probability, Husimi distributions, and a photon field decorrelator.
important_parameters: Spin magnitude (S), photon frequency (omega_c), photon loss rate (kappa), spin-cavity coupling (lambda), spin interaction (V), and tunneling (J).
main_assumptions: The use of the large-spin limit (S to infinity) for classical dynamics and the decoupling approximation for deriving classical equations of motion.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the physical schematic and phase diagram; Fig 2 illustrates the decay of photon number and synchronization dynamics; Fig 3 demonstrates the two types of quantum scarring through survival probability and Husimi distributions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the OCTD model and its physical realization, performs a classical stability and phase analysis, presents quantum simulation results regarding synchronization and chaos regulation, details the identification of two distinct scarring phenomena, and concludes with the connection to quantum tunneling.
why_interesting: It provides deep insights into how engineered dissipation can be used to control many-body dynamics, specifically by stabilizing synchronization and regulating chaos in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of a two-component Bose-Josephson junction (BJJ) inside a cavity with photon frequency ωc and loss rate κ. (b) Phase diagram in λ−V plane for κ = 0.3, sum- marizing different dynamical regimes and fixed points. The stable regime of FSR2 for the isolated case κ = 0 is marked by blue star-filled area. The scar of unstable FSR2 in this work is shown at the coupling strength marked by red dot in (b). (c) Configuration of the fixed points schematically. (d) Dynamics of photonic decorrelator Dph exhibiting transient chaos in region II of panel (b). (e) Pictorial representation of synchronization and dissipative scarring phenomena.

FIG. 2. Synchronization and transient chaos: Dynam- ics of (a,c) average photon number ⟨ˆn⟩, (b,d) relative phase ⟨ˆϕ+⟩and ⟨ˆz+⟩for single quantum trajectory corresponding to region-I (a,b) at V = 0.5, λ = 0.5, and region-II (c,d) at V = 1.8, λ = 0.2 of the phase diagram in Fig.1(b), respec- tively. Parameter chosen: κ = 0.3, S = 6 and sufficient num- ber of Fock states to minimize truncation error. In this and the rest of the figures, we set ℏ= 1, measure energy (time) in units of J(1/J) and choose ωc = 1, J = 1.

FIG. 3. Quantum scarring phenomena of unstable NP1: (a) Dynamics of survival probability Tr(ρ(t)ρ(0)) and (b,c,d) Husimi distribution Q of reduced density matrix of one of the spins for three different times at V = 1.5, λ = 0.5. The purple and red lines correspond to the initial state representing a generic phase space point and unstable NP1, respectively. Quantum scarring phenomena of unstable FSR2: (e) Dynamics of the overlap corresponding to two branches of unstable FSR2 Fj(t) [see the main text], where (red, green) lines for j = 1 and blue line for j = 2.
